title: Subnetting Calculations and Problem-Solving
description: Master binary subnetting methods, perform complex subnet calculations, and design optimal subnet schemes using multiple calculation techniques for real-world network problems.
starterCode:
# SUBNETTING CALCULATIONS EXERCISE
# Advanced binary methods and practical problem-solving

## SCENARIO: GlobalTech Solutions - Complex Network Design
## Multi-site corporation needing sophisticated subnetting
## Current allocation: 10.0.0.0/8 (16.7 million hosts)
## Challenge: Efficiently allocate subnets across HQ, 3 regions, 25 branches

## PART 1: BINARY SUBNETTING METHOD
## Q1: Binary method step 1 - Determine required subnets
##     Scenario: Need 6 equal subnets from 192.168.0.0/24
##     Formula: 2^n >= required subnets
##     2^2 = 4 (not enough)
##     2^3 = _______ (sufficient for 6 subnets)
##     Must borrow _______ bits from host portion

## Q2: Calculate new prefix length
##     Original: 192.168.0.0/24
##     Borrowed bits: 3
##     New prefix: /24 + 3 = /_______
##     New subnet mask: 255.255.255._______
##     (Binary last octet: 11100000)

## Q3: Binary subnet representation
##     /27 in the last octet:
##     Bits to work with: 11100000
##     This means: _______ block size
##     Subnets created: _______
##     Host bits remaining: _______ (32 - 27)
##     Usable hosts per subnet: 2^_______ - 2 = _______

## Q4: List all subnets from binary breakdown
##     Network 192.168.0.0/27:
##     Subnet 0 (00000000): 192.168.0._______/27
##     Subnet 1 (00100000 = 32): 192.168.0.32/27
##     Subnet 2 (01000000 = _______ ): 192.168.0._______/27
##     Subnet 3 (01100000 = _______ ): 192.168.0._______/27
##     Subnet 4 (10000000 = _______ ): 192.168.0._______/27
##     Subnet 5 (10100000 = _______ ): 192.168.0._______/27
##     Subnet 6 (11000000 = _______ ): 192.168.0._______/27
##     Subnet 7 (11100000 = 224): 192.168.0.224/27

## Q5: Complex requirement calculation
##     Problem: Subnet 172.16.0.0/16 into 1000 subnets
##     Formula: 2^n >= 1000
##     2^9 = 512 (not enough)
##     2^10 = _______ (sufficient) ✓
##     Must borrow _______ bits from /16
##     New prefix: /16 + 10 = /_______
##     New mask: 255.255._______._______
##     Total subnets: _______ (2^10)
##     Hosts per subnet: 2^_______ - 2 = _______ usable hosts

## PART 2: SUBNET ALLOCATION FROM HOST COUNT
## Q6: Reverse calculation - hosts to subnet
##     Need to support 100 hosts in one subnet:
##     Minimum host bits: log2(100+2) = log2(102) ≈ _______ bits
##     Try /25: 2^7 = 128 hosts, 126 usable ✓
##     Try /26: 2^6 = _______ hosts, 62 usable (too small)
##     Therefore: Use _______ for 100 hosts

## Q7: Multiple subnet allocation
##     Need subnets for: 200, 150, 75, 50, 25 hosts
##     200 hosts: /_______ (256 addresses)
##     150 hosts: /_______ (128 addresses)
##     75 hosts: /_______ (128 addresses)
##     50 hosts: /_______ (64 addresses)
##     25 hosts: /_______ (32 addresses)
##     
##     All these masks are _______ (all /24 or larger)

## Q8: Point-to-point link sizing
##     Router-to-router connection needs:
##     Minimum addresses: _______ (2 routers + network + broadcast = 4)
##     Subnet mask: /_______ 
##     Block size: _______
##     Usable IPs: _______
##     Example: 10.0.0.0/30 (routers at .1 and .2)
##     Example: 10.0.0.4/30 (routers at .5 and .6)

## PART 3: GLOBALTECH SCENARIO
## Q9: GlobalTech HQ allocation
##     HQ: 10.0.0.0/16 needs to support 3000 hosts
##     Calculation:
##     Need host bits: log2(3000+2) ≈ _______ bits
##     Try /19: 2^13 = _______ hosts ✓
##     Allocation: 10.0.0.0/_______
##     Next available: 10.0.32.0/16 onwards (32 = 2^5, /19 borrowed 5 bits)

## Q10: GlobalTech regions allocation
##      After HQ uses 10.0.0.0/19, remaining: 10.0.32.0/19 onwards
##      3 regions need equal allocation:
##      Bits needed for 3: 2^2 = 4 regions (enough)
##      Allocate from remaining /19 space:
##      Region 1 (East): 10.0.32.0/21 (2048 hosts)
##      Region 2 (West): 10.0.40.0/21 (2048 hosts)
##      Region 3 (Central): 10.0.48.0/21 (2048 hosts)
##      
##      Between Region 1 and 2:
##      /21 block size: 2^(23-21) = 2^2 = _______ in 3rd octet
##      Region 1: 10.0.32.0 to 10.0.39.255
##      Region 2: 10.0.40.0 to 10.0.47.255 ✓

## Q11: GlobalTech branch offices
##      25 branch offices, each needs 100 hosts
##      100 hosts = _______ (size needed)
##      Available space after regions: 10.0.56.0/16 onwards
##      Per branch: /_______ 
##      Need: 2^5 = _______ branches (25 branches fit in 32)
##      
##      Branch allocation (/24 each):
##      Branch 1: 10.0.56.0/24
##      Branch 2: 10.0.57.0/24
##      Branch 3: 10.0.58.0/24
##      ...
##      Branch 25: 10.0.80.0/24 (56 + 24 = 80)

## PART 4: SEVEN-SECOND METHOD
## Q12: Memorized quick reference
##      Mask Value | Block Size | Subnets | Usable Hosts
##      /25: 128   | 128        | 2       | _______
##      /26: 192   | 64         | 4       | _______
##      /27: 224   | 32         | 8       | _______
##      /28: 240   | 16         | 16      | _______
##      /29: 248   | 8          | 32      | _______
##      /30: 252   | 4          | 64      | _______ ✓ (router links)

## Q13: Quick mask calculation
##      Mask 255.255.255.224 (last octet = 224)
##      Binary: 11100000
##      Count 1s: _______ ones in /27 total
##      Block: 256 - 224 = _______
##      Subnets in /24: 256 / 32 = _______
##      Hosts: 32 - 2 = _______

## Q14: Seven-second drill
##      Given 192.168.1.0/24, subnet into 4 equal pieces:
##      Need /_______ (4 = 2^2)
##      Mask: 255.255.255._______ (192 = 11000000)
##      Block size: _______
##      Subnet 1: 192.168.1.0/26
##      Subnet 2: 192.168.1.64/26
##      Subnet 3: 192.168.1.128/26
##      Subnet 4: 192.168.1.192/26 ✓

## PART 5: TROUBLESHOOTING
## Q15: Overlapping subnet detection
##      Admin created:
##      Subnet A: 172.16.0.0/24 (0.0 to 0.255)
##      Subnet B: 172.16.0.128/25 (0.128 to 0.255)
##      Problem: _______ subnets _______ (overlap in 0.128-0.255)
##      Solution: Use either 172.16.0.0/24 and 172.16.1.0/24, OR
##               use 172.16.0.0/25 and 172.16.0.128/25

## Q16: Insufficient space
##      Network 192.168.0.0/24 allocated as:
##      Dept A: 192.168.0.0/25 (126 hosts) ✓
##      Dept B: 192.168.0.128/25 (126 hosts) ✓
##      New Dept C needs 50 hosts: Problem = _______
##      Reason: _______ address space available
##      Solution: _______ from larger network

## Q17: Asymmetric allocation
##      Need: 300 hosts, 200 hosts, 50 hosts, 10 hosts
##      /24 provides _______  hosts (254)
##      /25 provides _______ hosts (126)
##      /26 provides _______ hosts (62)
##      /27 provides _______ hosts (30)
##      /28 provides _______ hosts (14)
##      
##      Best allocation:
##      Dept A: 300 → _______ (/23 = 512)
##      Dept B: 200 → _______ (/24 = 256)
##      Dept C: 50 → _______ (/26 = 62)
##      Dept D: 10 → _______ (/28 = 14)

## Q18: Supernetting verification
##      Four /26 networks can be summarized:
##      192.168.0.0/26
##      192.168.0.64/26
##      192.168.0.128/26
##      192.168.0.192/26
##      Summary route: 192.168.0.0/_______
##      Reason: All fit in single /24 block ✓

## Q19: Rapid calculation scenario
##      Merge 172.16.0.0/25 and 172.16.0.128/25 into one:
##      Combined: 172.16.0.0/_______
##      Why: Both /25s together = one /24 ✓
##      Host count: _______ (126 + 126 + 2)

## Q20: Real-world complexity
##      ISP has 203.0.113.0/24 for customers:
##      Large customer: 100 hosts (203.0.113.0/25)
##      Medium customers: 30 hosts each (/26)
##      Small customers: 5 hosts each (/29)
##      
##      Allocate:
##      Large: 203.0.113.0/25 (0-127, 126 usable)
##      Medium 1: 203.0.113.128/26 (128-191)
##      Medium 2: 203.0.113.192/26 (192-223)
##      Remaining for small: 203.0.113.224-255
##      /29 size: _______ addresses (can fit _______ small customers)
solution:
# SUBNETTING CALCULATIONS SOLUTION

## PART 1: BINARY SUBNETTING METHOD

### Step-by-Step Binary Process

**Q1: Determine required subnets**

Scenario: Need 6 equal subnets from 192.168.0.0/24
Formula: 2^n >= required subnets
2^2 = 4 (not enough)
2^3 = **8** (sufficient for 6 subnets) ✓
Must borrow **3** bits from host portion

**Q2: Calculate new prefix length**

Original: 192.168.0.0/24
Borrowed bits: 3
New prefix: /24 + 3 = **/27**
New subnet mask: 255.255.255.**224**
(Binary last octet: 11100000 = 128+64+32 = 224)

**Q3: Binary subnet representation**

/27 in the last octet:
Bits to work with: 11100000
This means: **32** block size (256/8)
Subnets created: **8** (2^3)
Host bits remaining: **5** (32 - 27) ✓
Usable hosts per subnet: 2^**5** - 2 = **30** ✓

**Q4: List all subnets from binary breakdown**

Network 192.168.0.0/27:
Subnet 0 (00000000): 192.168.0.**0**/27
Subnet 1 (00100000 = 32): 192.168.0.32/27
Subnet 2 (01000000 = **64**): 192.168.0.**64**/27 ✓
Subnet 3 (01100000 = **96**): 192.168.0.**96**/27 ✓
Subnet 4 (10000000 = **128**): 192.168.0.**128**/27 ✓
Subnet 5 (10100000 = **160**): 192.168.0.**160**/27 ✓
Subnet 6 (11000000 = **192**): 192.168.0.**192**/27 ✓
Subnet 7 (11100000 = 224): 192.168.0.224/27

**Q5: Complex requirement calculation**

Problem: Subnet 172.16.0.0/16 into 1000 subnets
Formula: 2^n >= 1000
2^9 = 512 (not enough)
2^10 = **1024** (sufficient) ✓
Must borrow **10** bits from /16
New prefix: /16 + 10 = **/26**
New mask: 255.255.**255**.**192** (192 = 11000000)
Total subnets: **1024** (2^10) ✓
Hosts per subnet: 2^**6** - 2 = **62** usable hosts ✓

## PART 2: SUBNET ALLOCATION FROM HOST COUNT

### Reverse Calculation

**Q6: Reverse calculation - hosts to subnet**

Need to support 100 hosts in one subnet:
Minimum host bits: log2(100+2) = log2(102) ≈ **7** bits
Try /25: 2^7 = 128 hosts, 126 usable ✓
Try /26: 2^6 = **64** hosts, 62 usable (too small)
Therefore: Use **/25** for 100 hosts ✓

**Q7: Multiple subnet allocation**

Need subnets for: 200, 150, 75, 50, 25 hosts
200 hosts: **/24** (256 addresses) ✓
150 hosts: **/24** (128 addresses) ✓
75 hosts: **/25** (128 addresses) ✓
50 hosts: **/26** (64 addresses) ✓
25 hosts: **/26** (32 addresses) ✓

All these masks are **different** (uses VLSM - Variable Length Subnet Mask) ✓

**Q8: Point-to-point link sizing**

Router-to-router connection needs:
Minimum addresses: **4** (2 routers + network + broadcast = 4)
Subnet mask: **/30** ✓
Block size: **4** (256 - 252)
Usable IPs: **2** (routers at .1 and .2) ✓
Example: 10.0.0.0/30 (routers at .1 and .2)
Example: 10.0.0.4/30 (routers at .5 and .6)

## PART 3: GLOBALTECH SCENARIO

### Complex Real-World Implementation

**Q9: GlobalTech HQ allocation**

HQ: 10.0.0.0/16 needs to support 3000 hosts
Calculation:
Need host bits: log2(3000+2) ≈ **12** bits
Try /19: 2^13 = **8192** hosts ✓
Allocation: 10.0.0.0/**/19** ✓
Next available: 10.0.32.0/16 onwards (32 = 2^5, /19 borrowed 5 bits from /16)

**Q10: GlobalTech regions allocation**

After HQ uses 10.0.0.0/19, remaining: 10.0.32.0/19 onwards
3 regions need equal allocation:
Bits needed for 3: 2^2 = 4 regions (enough)
Allocate from remaining /19 space:
Region 1 (East): 10.0.32.0/21 (2048 hosts)
Region 2 (West): 10.0.40.0/21 (2048 hosts)
Region 3 (Central): 10.0.48.0/21 (2048 hosts)

Between Region 1 and 2:
/21 block size: 2^(23-21) = 2^2 = **4** in 3rd octet ✓
Region 1: 10.0.32.0 to 10.0.39.255
Region 2: 10.0.40.0 to 10.0.47.255 ✓

**Q11: GlobalTech branch offices**

25 branch offices, each needs 100 hosts
100 hosts = **/24** (size needed, provides 254 hosts)
Available space after regions: 10.0.56.0/16 onwards
Per branch: **/24** ✓
Need: 2^5 = **32** branches (25 branches fit in 32) ✓

Branch allocation (/24 each):
Branch 1: 10.0.56.0/24
Branch 2: 10.0.57.0/24
Branch 3: 10.0.58.0/24
...
Branch 25: 10.0.80.0/24 (56 + 24 = 80) ✓

## PART 4: SEVEN-SECOND METHOD

### Memorized Quick Reference

**Q12: Memorized quick reference**

| Mask Value | Block Size | Subnets | Usable Hosts |
|-----------|-----------|---------|----------------|
| /25: 128 | 128 | 2 | **126** ✓ |
| /26: 192 | 64 | 4 | **62** ✓ |
| /27: 224 | 32 | 8 | **30** ✓ |
| /28: 240 | 16 | 16 | **14** ✓ |
| /29: 248 | 8 | 32 | **6** ✓ |
| /30: 252 | 4 | 64 | **2** ✓ (router links) |

**Q13: Quick mask calculation**

Mask 255.255.255.224 (last octet = 224)
Binary: 11100000
Count 1s: **27** ones in /27 total (24 from first 3 octets + 3 in last)
Block: 256 - 224 = **32** ✓
Subnets in /24: 256 / 32 = **8** ✓
Hosts: 32 - 2 = **30** ✓

**Q14: Seven-second drill**

Given 192.168.1.0/24, subnet into 4 equal pieces:
Need **/26** (4 = 2^2, so borrow 2 bits) ✓
Mask: 255.255.255.**192** (192 = 11000000)
Block size: **64** ✓
Subnet 1: 192.168.1.0/26
Subnet 2: 192.168.1.64/26
Subnet 3: 192.168.1.128/26
Subnet 4: 192.168.1.192/26 ✓

## PART 5: TROUBLESHOOTING

### Common Mistakes and Solutions

**Q15: Overlapping subnet detection**

Admin created:
Subnet A: 172.16.0.0/24 (0.0 to 0.255)
Subnet B: 172.16.0.128/25 (0.128 to 0.255)
Problem: **Two** subnets **overlap** (overlap in 0.128-0.255) ⚠️
Solution: Use either 172.16.0.0/24 and 172.16.1.0/24, OR
         use 172.16.0.0/25 and 172.16.0.128/25 ✓

**Q16: Insufficient space**

Network 192.168.0.0/24 allocated as:
Dept A: 192.168.0.0/25 (126 hosts) ✓
Dept B: 192.168.0.128/25 (126 hosts) ✓
New Dept C needs 50 hosts: Problem = **No space available** ⚠️
Reason: **All** address space available (no room for third subnet)
Solution: **Redesign** from larger network ✓

**Q17: Asymmetric allocation**

Need: 300 hosts, 200 hosts, 50 hosts, 10 hosts
/24 provides **254** hosts
/25 provides **126** hosts
/26 provides **62** hosts
/27 provides **30** hosts
/28 provides **14** hosts

Best allocation (VLSM - right-size each):
Dept A: 300 → **/23** (/23 = 512) ✓
Dept B: 200 → **/24** (/24 = 256) ✓
Dept C: 50 → **/26** (/26 = 62) ✓
Dept D: 10 → **/28** (/28 = 14) ✓

**Q18: Supernetting verification**

Four /26 networks can be summarized:
192.168.0.0/26
192.168.0.64/26
192.168.0.128/26
192.168.0.192/26
Summary route: 192.168.0.0/**/24** ✓
Reason: All fit in single /24 block ✓

**Q19: Rapid calculation scenario**

Merge 172.16.0.0/25 and 172.16.0.128/25 into one:
Combined: 172.16.0.0/**/24** ✓
Why: Both /25s together = one /24 ✓
Host count: **254** (126 + 126 + 2) ✓

**Q20: Real-world complexity**

ISP has 203.0.113.0/24 for customers:
Large customer: 100 hosts (203.0.113.0/25)
Medium customers: 30 hosts each (/26)
Small customers: 5 hosts each (/29)

Allocate:
Large: 203.0.113.0/25 (0-127, 126 usable) ✓
Medium 1: 203.0.113.128/26 (128-191) ✓
Medium 2: 203.0.113.192/26 (192-223) ✓
Remaining for small: 203.0.113.224-255
/29 size: **8** addresses (can fit **3** small customers) ✓
(224-231, 232-239, 240-247)
hints: Binary method: Borrow bits from host portion using 2^n formula to get required subnets
Step 1: Determine bits needed; Step 2: Calculate new prefix; Step 3: Convert to binary; Step 4: List subnets
Block size = 256 - mask_value; determines spacing between subnets in rapid calculation
To allocate subnets by host count, find minimum host bits needed: log2(hosts+2), round up
/30 is special-case for point-to-point: only 4 addresses = 2 usable; used for router links
VLSM = Variable Length Subnet Mask; allows different subnet sizes in same network for efficiency
Overlapping subnets = design error; verify addresses don't overlap before deploying
Route summarization (supernetting): Multiple small subnets can often be advertised as one larger block
Real-world networks rarely use fixed-length subnetting; VLSM with right-sized subnets is standard

testCases.description: Should demonstrate the binary method for subnet calculations
testCases.validation: assert ('3' in solution or '27' in solution or 'borrow' in solution.lower())

testCases.description: Should calculate a subnetting scheme that accommodates 1000 or more subnets
testCases.validation: assert ('/26' in solution or '1024' in solution or '62' in solution)
assert ('/25' in solution or '126' in solution or '100' in solution)

testCases.description: Should correctly identify point-to-point link subnet sizing using /30 or /31 masks
testCases.validation: assert ('/30' in solution or '2' in solution or 'router' in solution.lower())

testCases.description: Should properly allocate IP address space for headquarters network requirements
testCases.validation: assert ('/19' in solution or '8192' in solution or 'hq' in solution.lower())

testCases.description: Should correctly assign regional IP address blocks using hierarchical addressing
testCases.validation: assert ('4' in solution or '/21' in solution or 'region' in solution.lower())

testCases.description: Should design a subnetting scheme that accommodates 25 branch office networks
testCases.validation: assert ('/24' in solution or '32' in solution or 'branch' in solution.lower())

testCases.description: Should correctly explain overlapping subnets concepts and implementation details
testCases.validation: assert ('overlap' in solution.lower() or 'error' in solution.lower())

testCases.description: Should correctly explain vlsm right-sizing concepts and implementation details
testCases.validation: assert ('/23' in solution or '/24' in solution or 'vlsm' in solution.lower())

testCases.description: Should correctly explain route summarization concepts and implementation details
testCases.validation: assert ('/24' in solution or 'summar' in solution.lower())
